g.data-narration: Welcome! In this lecture, we'll explore client-server architecture, the foundation of modern distributed computing systems.
Client-server architecture is a distributed model where clients request services and servers provide them. This asymmetric relationship is fundamental.
g.text: A distributed computing model that partitions tasks between

g.data-narration: A client is a front-end application that initiates requests. A server is a back-end system that processes requests and returns responses.
Property 1: Asymmetric Communication. Clients initiate requests while servers respond reactively. This asymmetry distinguishes it from peer-to-peer.
g.text: Client initiates requests; Server responds reactively. Unlike peer-to-peer where
all nodes can initiate and respond equally.

g.data-narration: Property 2: Resource Sharing. Multiple clients access shared server resources including databases, files, and services. This centralization ensures data consistency.
g.text: Multiple clients access centralized server resources: databases, files, printers,
computational services. Centralized management simplifies administration.

g.data-narration: Property 3: Scalability. Systems can scale vertically by upgrading hardware, or horizontally by adding more servers. Load balancers distribute traffic across servers.
g.text: Vertical: Upgrade server hardware | Horizontal: Add more servers
Load balancers distribute requests across multiple server instances.

g.data-narration: Property 4: Centralized Management. The server is the central point for managing applications, data, and security. Changes propagate to all clients automatically.
g.text: Server is central point for applications, data, security policies, business logic.
Server-side changes automatically propagate to all clients.

g.data-narration: Property 5: Statelessness. HTTP is stateless - each request is independent. Servers don't remember previous requests. Sessions and cookies maintain state.
g.text: HTTP is stateless: each request is independent. Server doesn't retain client info.
Sessions, cookies, and tokens maintain state across requests.

g.data-narration: Property 6: Protocol-Based Communication. Clients and servers communicate using standardized protocols like HTTP, FTP, SMTP. This enables interoperability.
g.text: Standardized protocols enable interoperability between clients and servers.

g.data-narration: Property 7: Concurrency Handling. Servers handle multiple clients simultaneously using multi-threading, thread pools, and asynchronous processing.
g.text: Servers handle multiple simultaneous client requests through multi-threading,
thread pools, connection pooling, and non-blocking I/O.

g.data-narration: Thin clients rely on servers for processing (like terminals). Thick clients perform significant local processing (like desktop apps).
g.text: Local processing + Server for storage (Desktop Apps)

g.data-narration: Three-tier architecture separates presentation, application logic, and data layers. This enhances maintainability and allows independent scaling.
g.text: Three distinct layers: Presentation (Client) | Application Logic (App Server) | Data (Database)

g.data-narration: Here's a real example: when you visit a website, your browser sends an HTTP request to the web server, which queries the database and returns an HTML response.
Load balancers distribute requests across multiple servers. If one server fails, others handle the load. This provides scalability and fault tolerance.
g.text: Distributes traffic across multiple servers for scalability and fault tolerance.

g.data-narration: To summarize: Client-server architecture has 7 key properties including asymmetric communication, resource sharing, scalability, centralized management, statelessness, protocol-based communication, and concurrency handling.
g.text: Key Takeaways: 7 Properties of Client-Server Architecture
Asymmetric Communication | Resource Sharing | Scalability | Centralized Management | Statelessness | Protocol-Based | Concurrency
